CLOSURE_REMARKS: Errors self recovered. We experienced an issue between 8 PM to 9 PM EST where the DMS_retrieveMtnLevelAttributeDetails_ALT query was returning incomplete data, while storing the data in cassandra by fetching from AEM in scheduler service, the request got timed out, data was not available for 1 hour. When the delta load happened it was resolved. When data is not available we are adding a fallback to to read the content from AEM so user experience is not impacted.

INCIDENT_REASON: Reason: 300% more traffic is observed compared to past window average traffic - 28095.0;System: CV9V ,DC: AWS-E ,MetricName: traffic ,Application: SOE-Static-Akamai-ASSETS.example.com
CLOSURE_REMARKS: There was a connectivity issue overnight between Akamai DataStream and our ingestion endpoint. The logs piled up in a buffer. Since our dashboards use "Ingestion Time" (@AkamaiEventTime) instead of the actual "Event Time" (reqTimeSec), it stacked hours of traffic into a single 10-minute window. The data is all there, just plotted at the wrong time. The alerts will stop once the logs backlogs clears.

INCIDENT_REASON: Reason: ibmmqqdepth_TDCqueues >= 32000.0;System: DAGV ,DC: BDC ,MetricName: ibmmqqdepth_TDCqueues ,Application: CPF_MQ-IBMMQ, Host: tdclpdagva149.bdc.mydomain.com
CLOSURE_REMARKS: Consumer restarted on <tdclpdagva184.example.com>, instance <CPF jvms> using Jenkins job https://jenkins-vcg2.vpc.example.com/vcg2/job/VZW.DAGV.MCS.PROD.GRID/job/VZW.DAGV.MCS.PROD.CPF_Springboot_BDC_Cassandra/ CPF Qdepths were not draining even after multiple restarts. Logs were reviewed and the following error was observed: "Caused by: com.datastax.driver.core.exceptions.ReadTimeoutException: Cassandra timeout during read query at consistency ALL (12 responses were required but only 11 replica responded)". Cassandra team engaged and confirmed that all the clusters are healthy no issues. PNO team determined it might be due to bulk batch processing. SRE restarted consumer and Qdepth cleared.

INCIDENT_REASON: Reason: CW_WriteIOPS >= 15001.0;System: G1GV ,DC: AWS-E ,MetricName: CW_WriteIOPS ,Application: Databases-MVD, Host: rddprde
CLOSURE_REMARKS: https://onejira.example.com/browse/GTSGR-426312 177017612700279 [9:18 AM]https://support.console.aws.amazon.com/support/home#/case/?displayId=177017612700279&language=en we open the AWS case and they provide the short team and long term resolution , created the jira and assign to the pct 196 as of now team is working on it Immediate solution 1. CloudWatch metrics reveal that IOPS and throughput are experiencing throttling. I recommend increasing these values to address the elevated requirements. You can leverage CloudWatch to monitor usage over a longer duration (e.g., over a month) to determine appropriate target values for provisioning. [11:55 AM]Long tem solution If the average wait time for a SQL is low, but the number of waits is high, then the application might be committing after every row, rather than batching COMMITs. So one way to reduce this is by issuing COMMINT/ROLLBACK in batches. When the LGWR wants to flush the log-buffer content to Redo-log and if it finds Redo log is not free or not completed flushing to Archive-log then LGWR wait for the redo-log to get free thereby increasing the wait time. This can handled by adding Redo-logs and increase the size as well. Though I can see you have around 26 redo logs and each one is 4 GB, however, there were frequent redo switch observed indicating a high write heavy application. Check the SQL-Support-ID indicated above, you may need to engage with your DBA team to tune it. In case the SQL is a "COMMIT", it confirms the second point above.

INCIDENT_REASON: Reason: prc correlation id is invalid >= 2.0;System:B6NV, DC:AWS-W , MetricName: prc correlation id is invalid , Application: SOE-CPC-Services-Cpc-Service
CLOSURE_REMARKS: Earlier today, we observed a significant spike in "prc correlation id is invalid in OMS" errors on B6NV-SOE-CPC-Services in AWS West. This issue directly impacted ACSS and Field operations. Upon analysis with the DVS and ORBPM application teams, we identified that LT Servers were incorrectly brought into rotation. These servers began processing traffic as flows were routed to these specific IPs rather than the intended hosts. Current Status: Action Taken: We have disabled the offending IPs. Result: Error rates have recovered, and services are currently stable. Since there were no planned changes or deployments scheduled for last night, we need to understand how these IPs were introduced into the rotation. Could you please investigate this incident and provide an update on the root cause?
